metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify or create the concrete function(s) in the existing codebase that `JiraBackend.IdentityConvention` will delegate to for stamp-and-find (e.g., extract a `_stamp_rebar_id_label` helper from `dispatch_one.py`/`binding_store.py`), or explicitly call out that `IdentityConvention` is stub-only in `JiraBackend` at this story's scope (deferred to S4).
evidence: dispatch_one.py:320: `_call_with_retry(client.add_label, jira_key, f'rebar-id:{local_id}')` — stamp logic inline
binding_store.py:516: `client.add_label(keyed, f'rebar-id:{local_id}')` — stamp logic inline
binding_walk.py:341: label prefix check `for prefix in ('rebar-id:', 'rebar-id-')` — find logic inline
apply_inbound_records.py:223: `_call_with_retry(client.add_label, jira_key, f'rebar-id:{local_id}')` — stamp logic inline
No single function like `_stamp_rebar_id` or `_find_rebar_id` exists in the codebase to serve as JiraBackend's delegation target for IdentityConvention
findings.finding: The plan states `IdentityConvention ← the rebar-id label read/write convention, expressed over RemoteRef`, but unlike the other four Protocols (which delegate to a single existing function), the rebar-id label stamping/finding behavior is distributed across at least four core modules (`dispatch_one.py:320`, `binding_store.py:516,522`, `apply_inbound_records.py:223`, `binding_walk.py:341`). No single wrappable function exists for JiraBackend to delegate to for `IdentityConvention`, making the 'pure delegation shim' claim for this Protocol unverifiable without additional design work.
findings.location: Approach section: 'IdentityConvention ← the rebar-id label read/write convention, expressed over RemoteRef'; also Testing section: 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'
scenarios: Implementer opens the story and cannot find a single function to wrap for IdentityConvention; must either introduce new helper functions (behavior change, contradicting 'no logic moves') or leave IdentityConvention as a thin facade over inline code that does not yet exist as a delegatable unit.
Contract test 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef' cannot be driven purely via JiraBackend delegation if the delegation target does not exist as a callable unit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the capability-advertisement detection mechanism co-located with the opt-in Protocol definitions in `_backend.py` — e.g. state explicitly whether callers use `isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)`, a `backend.capabilities()` method, or another mechanism, so the contract suite and FakeBackend can be implemented consistently.
evidence: Plan Testing section: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'

The _backend.py edit-set describes the three opt-in Protocols by name but gives no method or attribute on Backend or on the capability Protocols themselves that a dispatcher could call to decide whether to invoke them

The five role Protocols each have their method signatures grounded in named modules (e.g. TicketTransport ← acli.AcliClient methods), but no analogous grounding is given for the capability-advertisement mechanism
ABSENCE: no `capabilities()`, `supports()`, `has_capability()`, or isinstance-dispatch rule is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) are named and the contract tests are described as 'exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior)', but the DETECTION MECHANISM — the decision rule by which a caller (or the contract suite) determines whether a given Backend instance advertises an opt-in capability — is never specified anywhere in the plan. The plan does not state whether detection uses isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks), hasattr, a capabilities() method on Backend, or some other protocol. This decision rule must be co-located with the _backend.py schema definition; without it, an implementer of FakeBackend and a reviewer of the contract suite cannot know what 'observed via behavior' actually means at the code level.
findings.location: Testing section — 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'; AC line 2 (contract suite); _backend.py edit-set
scenarios: Implementer writes FakeBackend without the opt-in Protocols and the contract suite tries isinstance(fake, SupportsLinks) — fails because the detection mechanism wasn't specified and FakeBackend author chose a different shape

JiraBackend author implements SupportsLinks as a mixin but the contract suite uses hasattr detection — passes accidentally; a second backend that omits links silently skips the suite rather than asserting non-support
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the _backend.py edit-set description: 'Callers detect opt-in capability via isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks) / isinstance(backend, SupportsComments) / isinstance(backend, SupportsIncremental); no separate capabilities() method is needed.' Then note in the contract suite section that FakeBackend selectively inherits the opt-in Protocol to test the guarded path, and omits it to test the skip path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` is assigned to `FieldSanitizer` (not `OutboundMapper`) in `_backend.py`, since the actual call sites are `_diff_fields` (comparison path) and `_sanitize_description` (which already lives in the `FieldSanitizer` source module `adapters/jira/jira_fields.py`) — not within `_map_local_to_jira_fields` itself.
evidence: outbound_fields.py:567: `local_val = _load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val)` is inside `_diff_fields`, not `_map_local_to_jira_fields`
inbound_differ.py:192: `local_val = _load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val)` — called in the inbound differ, not the outbound mapper

adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:18: `from rebar_reconciler.adf import fit_text_to_adf_limit as _fit_description_to_adf_limit` — imported into the FieldSanitizer's home module and used by `_sanitize_description`
Plan principle: 'Derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls'
findings.finding: The plan places `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` under `OutboundMapper` (`OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)`), but the actual codebase shows `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is NOT called inside `_map_local_to_jira_fields` — it is called from `_diff_fields` (the differ/comparison path at `outbound_fields.py:567`) and from `inbound_differ.py:192`. The plan's own stated derivation principle is to 'derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls', which would place `fit_text_to_adf_limit` in `FieldSanitizer` (already backed by `_sanitize_description`, which itself calls `fit_text_to_adf_limit`), not in `OutboundMapper`. Misattributing it to `OutboundMapper` would produce a Protocol that doesn't match actual call-site boundaries, complicating the S4 rewiring where sites call `fit_text_to_adf_limit` via the FieldSanitizer path (description send-side truncation) rather than the mapper path.
scenarios: When implementing `OutboundMapper.map_local_to_jira` the implementer will look at `_map_local_to_jira_fields` and not find `fit_text_to_adf_limit` there — the Protocol signature will either be missing the method or placed in the wrong role.

In S4 rewiring, sites that call `fit_text_to_adf_limit` (the differ paths) will be routed to `OutboundMapper` rather than `FieldSanitizer`, creating a semantically wrong dependency: the inbound differ would need to call the outbound mapper for a FieldSanitizer operation.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach bullet to: `OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields` (remove `+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit`); add `fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `FieldSanitizer` alongside `_sanitize_description` (which already delegates to it), consistent with `adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:18` importing it for that purpose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the Approach to state that `InboundMapper` is derived from `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields` (the pure differ-level mapper) only, and that `inbound_translate` applier helpers (`_jira_key_to_local_id`, `_jira_status_to_local`) are NOT part of the `InboundMapper` Protocol at this story's scope.
evidence: inbound_fields.py:141: `def _map_jira_to_local_fields(jira_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]` — the actual mapper, pure function, no I/O
inbound_translate.py:1-23: docstring says 'Pure translation + local-event-store IO helpers used by the inbound leaf appliers' — it is an applier-path module, not a differ/mapper module
inbound_translate._jira_key_to_local_id, _jira_status_to_local — translation helpers used in apply_inbound.py and apply_inbound_records.py, not in inbound_differ.py's mapper path
apply_planning.py:27: `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate import _jira_key_to_local_id` — applier-path usage
findings.finding: The plan attributes `InboundMapper` to `'the inbound_fields/inbound_translate payload→local extraction'`, but `inbound_translate` is primarily an applier-path event-write module (it writes local ticket event files) — not a mapper. Its translation helpers (`_jira_status_to_local`, `_jira_key_to_local_id`, `_resolve_priority`) are used by the inbound applier, not by the differ's mapper. The actual differ-level mapper (`_map_jira_to_local_fields`) lives entirely in `inbound_fields.py`. Conflating `inbound_translate` as an `InboundMapper` source may cause the Protocol's method signatures to include applier-path I/O functions that belong to a different role (or no Protocol at all at this story's scope).
findings.location: Approach section: 'InboundMapper ← the inbound_fields/inbound_translate payload→local extraction'
scenarios: Implementer includes `_jira_key_to_local_id`/`_jira_status_to_local` in `InboundMapper`'s Protocol signature; these are applier-path helpers that have nothing to do with the differ mapper, polluting the Protocol boundary.

Contract test for `InboundMapper` round-trips tries to call inbound_translate functions that perform I/O (write event files), making the 'pure mapper' contract test impossible to run without a repo root.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach bullet to: 'InboundMapper ← `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields` (the pure Jira→local field mapper used by the inbound differ)'; explicitly exclude `inbound_translate` I/O helpers from this Protocol.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering the 'Teeth' mutation step: e.g., 'A deliberate delegation omission in JiraBackend (e.g., dropping the priority map) causes at least one contract or characterization test to go RED — verified by performing and documenting the perturbation during story implementation.'
evidence: Testing section: 'Teeth: perturb a JiraBackend delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore.'
No AC mentions mutation testing, perturbation, or a RED-then-restore verification step.
findings.finding: The 'Teeth' process step — 'perturb a JiraBackend delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore' — is described work in the Testing section with no corresponding acceptance criterion that verifies it was performed.
findings.location: Testing section, 'Teeth' subsection; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
scenarios: An implementer skips the mutation step (which validates the tests actually have teeth) because no AC requires it, and the characterization tests are accepted as green without verifying they would catch regressions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend AC4's `git diff --name-only` guard to also allow `adapters/jira/__init__.py` (and optionally the rebar_reconciler package `__init__.py`) if those files need to export the new symbols, or explicitly state that no `__init__.py` exports are added in this story.
evidence: adapters/jira/__init__.py exists and currently lists only jira_fields.py as its content per the directory listing

AC1 specifies `from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler._backend import Backend, RemoteRef` — _backend.py is at the rebar_reconciler package root, not under adapters/jira/, so its __init__.py is not affected

However JiraBackend lives at `adapters/jira/backend.py` and callers in S4 will need `from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.backend import JiraBackend` — the adapters/jira/__init__.py may or may not need updating depending on import style chosen
findings.finding: AC4's `git diff --name-only` guard lists only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and `tests/` as touched files, but `adapters/jira/__init__.py` would likely need modification to export `JiraBackend` for the AC1 import path (`from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler._backend import ...`) to work correctly and for `JiraBackend` to be discoverable. This file is absent from the guard's enumeration, meaning the AC4 verification would incorrectly fail if `__init__.py` is updated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and files under `tests/`'
scenarios: If the implementer adds a JiraBackend re-export to `adapters/jira/__init__.py` (reasonable practice), the AC4 git-diff check will flag it as a violation even though it is a legitimate companion change.
If the AC4 guard is taken literally and __init__.py is not updated, downstream stories (S3/S4) may need to use the full dotted path import rather than the package-level name.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC4 to: `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, `adapters/jira/__init__.py` (if export added), and files under `tests/`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cardinality-asserting proving command to AC2, e.g. `pytest --collect-only -q tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py | grep -cE 'JiraBackend|FakeBackend'` and assert it outputs `2` (or 2×N where N is the number of contract tests), so both parameterizations are confirmed to have been collected and run.
evidence: 'verified by `make test` running the suite twice via the `backend_factory` fixture'
ABSENCE: no cardinality assertion confirms both variants appear in collected/run tests; this is verify-command defect taxonomy item (5) missing cardinality assertion
findings.finding: AC2's proving command (`make test` running the suite twice via the `backend_factory` fixture) does not guarantee both `JiraBackend` and `FakeBackend` parameterizations actually executed — a misconfigured fixture could silently skip one variant while `make test` still exits 0.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'JiraBackend implements Backend; the contract suite passes when parameterized over both JiraBackend... and FakeBackend — verified by make test running the suite twice via the backend_factory fixture'
scenarios: The `backend_factory` fixture is defined but only one parametrize value is registered; `make test` passes on only one backend with no failure.
A developer accidentally marks a parameterization as `pytest.mark.skip`; the AC is considered met because make test passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'Confirmed by `pytest --collect-only -q tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py 2>/dev/null | grep -cE "\[JiraBackend\]|\[FakeBackend\]"` returning a count ≥ 2 (one per contract test variant).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a base ref in the AC4 proving command so it is executable (e.g. `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD`) and optionally pipe through a grep/wc assertion to fail if unexpected files appear.
evidence: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and files under `tests/`'
ABSENCE: no base ref (branch name, commit SHA, or `origin/main`) is specified; the command is incomplete per verify-command defect taxonomy item (7) missing prerequisite-state ordering
findings.finding: The `git diff --name-only` proving command for AC4 omits the base ref, making it unexecutable as written — without a base like `main` or `HEAD~1`, the command produces no output or compares against the working tree only, depending on Git state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'No core differ/apply module is modified by this story — verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only...'
scenarios: An executor runs `git diff --name-only` against a dirty working tree and sees only unstaged changes, missing staged or committed story-scope changes.
A CI step runs `git diff --name-only` with no base and gets an empty result, falsely passing the AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 verification note with: `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD | grep -Ev '^(src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend\.py|src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend\.py|tests/)' | diff /dev/null -` (exits non-zero if any unexpected file appears).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the capability Protocol contract scope in the Testing section: at this story's boundary, the contract can only assert structural conformance (isinstance checks, method presence) and unit-level invocation — the behavioral invariant 'core never calls a capability method on a non-implementing backend' is only testable after S4 rewires the core dispatch loop; mark it as a deferred contract assertion to be extended in S4.
evidence: Plan explicitly scopes out core rewiring: 'Out of scope: rewiring any core call site or construction site (S4)'
S4 ticket (6673-7636-a116-4f90) confirms the core importers (outbound_differ, inbound_differ, applier, fetcher, run_differs, etc.) are not touched in this story
The core dispatch loop (applier.py:443/:605, fetcher.py:371, run_differs.py:403) still calls AcliClient directly after this story — there is no capability-check path yet
ABSENCE: no mechanism in _backend.py or JiraBackend can intercept a core module that hasn't been rewired yet
findings.finding: The capability Protocol contract as written ('observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links') cannot be verified as a behavioral contract in this story, because the core call sites that would honor the capability check are not rewired until S4. The contract suite runs JiraBackend and FakeBackend in isolation from the core dispatch loop, so it can only test structural isinstance() semantics at this stage. The plan's framing creates a false confidence signal: tests will be green but the true enforcement invariant remains untested until S4.
findings.location: Testing section — 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links)'
scenarios: A developer sees the green capability contract tests and concludes the enforcement is live, ships a FakeBackend that omits SupportsLinks, and is surprised when the core still calls sync_links on it in the next reconciler pass (because S4 hasn't landed yet)

The characterization of 'observed via behavior' misleads the implementer into writing a test that relies on an integration point (core dispatching through Backend) that doesn't exist in this story's edit set
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, rephrase: 'The capability Protocol contracts assert structural conformance only at this stage — isinstance(jira_backend, SupportsLinks) is True, isinstance(fake_backend_without_links, SupportsLinks) is False, and the opted-in methods are callable. The behavioral enforcement invariant (core never invokes a capability method on a non-implementing backend) is a deferred contract extension for S4, where the core dispatch loop is rewired.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient`, but `transition_issue` is NOT an instance method on `AcliClient` — only `transition_issue_by_name` is. The module-level `acli.transition_issue` (acli.py:122) is a standalone free function that constructs its own `AcliClient` internally and is not part of the `AcliClient` interface. This mismatch between the plan's claim and the actual `AcliClient` surface means the `TicketTransport` Protocol's transition signature will need to be derived from `transition_issue_by_name`, not from a method called `transition_issue`.
findings.location: Approach section — 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach text to read: `TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on AcliClient: create_issue, update_issue, get_issue, transition_issue_by_name, add_comment, get_comments (+ search_issues for the fetch path)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-parent this leaf from S1 (ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb, docs-only) to the epic (bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a), which is the actual containing contract for the S2 code deliverables (_backend.py, JiraBackend, contract tests); S1 is a prerequisite dependency, not a scope-container.
evidence: S1 (ef33) Scope: 'Revised §(d) … Status/Decision/Consequences edits … No code changes.'
S1 (ef33) file_impact: [{'path': 'docs/adr/0035-reconciler-vendor-adapter-seam.md', ...}] — a single docs file, no src/ or tests/ files
S1 (ef33) ACs: all four ACs verify doc text content or make lint on a docs-only change — none mention Backend, RemoteRef, JiraBackend, or any code artifact

Leaf Edit-set: 'New src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py', 'New src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py', 'New tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py' — code and tests, entirely outside S1's scope

Epic (bbf1) S2 slot: 'S2 ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798 — define the Backend Protocols + RemoteRef + a backend-agnostic contract suite + a thin JiraBackend (zero behavior change). No core rewiring.' — the leaf's work matches S2 under the epic, not under S1
findings.finding: The leaf's declared parent (S1, ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb) is a docs-only story whose entire scope is editing docs/adr/0035-reconciler-vendor-adapter-seam.md with no code changes; S1's ACs contain nothing about code deliverables. The leaf's substantive outputs — _backend.py (8 Protocols + RemoteRef), adapters/jira/backend.py (JiraBackend), and contract/characterization tests — lie entirely outside S1's declared scope. The leaf's work is properly described by the grandparent epic's S2 slot (bbf1 §'Child decomposition'), making the correct parent the epic bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a, not the docs-only S1 story.
findings.location: Context / Problem — 'the RemoteRef identity type pinned in ADR 0035 §(d) [rebar:ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb]'; leaf's parent_id resolves to ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb (S1)
scenarios: A reviewer applying the G7 containment rule against S1 as parent would correctly flag that _backend.py, JiraBackend, and all test files are out-of-scope for a docs-only parent, creating a false divergence signal on an otherwise well-scoped story.
If S1 is ever re-reviewed or its scope tightened, the leaf inherits the constraint incorrectly — locking code work to a docs story's boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Change parent_id from ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb to bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a (the epic). Optionally add ef33 as a deps entry (relation: 'depends_on') to preserve the sequencing signal that S1 must land before this story's code can be implemented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_backend.py` will ever be loaded standalone via `spec_from_file_location` (as is common in this test suite), and if so, replace `@dataclass` with `NamedTuple` for `RemoteRef` (the pattern already mandated in `invariants.py:107-112`) or explicitly document that `_backend.py` requires pre-registration in `sys.modules` before exec.
evidence: Plan: 'the `RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}` dataclass'

invariants.py:107-112: 'A NamedTuple (not a dataclass) deliberately: invariants.py is exec'd standalone in tests via spec_from_file_location WITHOUT registering the module in sys.modules, and on Python 3.14 @dataclass resolves stringified (PEP 563) annotations through sys.modules[cls.__module__] at class-definition time, which raises AttributeError for such an unregistered module. NamedTuple does not, so it is robust to every load path.'
__main__.py:79-90 documents the same constraint as a load-bearing Python 3.14 regression (bug 5be7 chain).
ABSENCE: the plan contains no note that _backend.py will avoid standalone spec_from_file_location loading or use NamedTuple instead of @dataclass for RemoteRef.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `RemoteRef` as a `@dataclass` without addressing a codebase-documented incompatibility: `@dataclass` in modules exec'd standalone via `spec_from_file_location` on Python 3.14 raises `AttributeError` because `sys.modules[cls.__module__]` returns `None` at class-definition time. The codebase already documents and works around this with `NamedTuple` in `invariants.py:107-112`. Since `_backend.py` sits at the package root (where test infrastructure routinely spec-loads modules by path), and the plan's test suite exercises `_backend.py` independently, this assertion is unverified and may silently fail under the existing test loader on Python 3.14.
findings.location: Edit-set / `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py` description; AC line 1 ('`RemoteRef{vendor=…, instance=…, remote_id=…}` constructs')
scenarios: A contract or characterization test loads _backend.py via spec_from_file_location (the same pattern used by dozens of existing tests in this package) — RemoteRef's @dataclass decorator fires at class-definition time, sys.modules[cls.__module__] is None on Python 3.14, and the test suite fails with AttributeError before any assertion runs.

The import-test AC ('RemoteRef(vendor=…, instance=…, remote_id=…) constructs') passes in normal package-import mode but silently masks the standalone-load failure until a test upgrades its load mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `RemoteRef` as a `NamedTuple` (matching the `invariants.py` precedent): `class RemoteRef(NamedTuple): vendor: str; instance: str; remote_id: str` — this is safe under every load path including `spec_from_file_location` without `sys.modules` registration on Python 3.14.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the Testing section (or AC3) the minimum set of local fields the round-trip contracts must cover (e.g. 'at minimum: summary, description, priority, labels, status, assignee, and the rebar-id label') and define what makes a fixture 'representative' (e.g. 'one create-path ticket and one update-path ticket from the existing test fixtures').
evidence: 'outbound map→inbound unmap round-trips to the same local fields the reconciler syncs' — 'the reconciler syncs' is defined only by consulting the codebase, not by the plan
'representative tickets' in AC3 — no enumeration of which tickets or which fields constitute 'representative'
ABSENCE: no fixture schema, field list, or minimum coverage enumeration is given
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate the specific local fields (or a named fixture set) that the round-trip contract tests and characterization tests must cover, leaving the executor to decide which fields constitute sufficient coverage — a defaultable but unstated design choice.
findings.location: Testing section — 'outbound map→inbound unmap round-trips to the same local fields the reconciler syncs'; Acceptance Criteria — AC3 'representative tickets'
scenarios: An executor writes characterization tests covering only 3 of 15 synced fields and considers the AC met, while a later story discovers regressions on uncovered fields.
Two executors produce different fixture sets, making the test suite non-reproducible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC #3's verification language: replace 'byte-for-byte identical' with 'structurally equal (assert actual == expected on the dict)' to match the 'diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty' verification method.
evidence: 'byte-for-byte identical' implies string/bytes-level comparison
'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty' implies structural dict equality
These two verification descriptions are not equivalent — dict key ordering, float precision, and nested structure all affect byte equality but not dict equality
findings.finding: AC #3 uses 'byte-for-byte identical' to describe dict comparison verified by 'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty' — these are different concepts (byte-level string equality vs. structural dict equality) and the mixed terminology could mislead an implementer into writing overly strict or wrong assertions (e.g. serialized string comparison instead of dict equality).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC #3: 'Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main for the representative fixtures — verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty).'
scenarios: An implementer writes `assert str(actual) == str(expected)` (byte-like) when `assert actual == expected` (dict equality) is intended, or vice versa, causing tests to be fragile or incorrectly specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main' with 'structurally identical to pre-story main (dict equality)' and remove the parenthetical about byte-for-byte, keeping only 'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR update (or new ADR section) documenting the Protocol taxonomy (five role + three capability), the `RemoteRef` canonical definition in `_backend.py`, and the contract-suite-as-backend-harness pattern — and link it from ADR 0035 §(d).
evidence: Plan cites 'ADR 0035 §(d) [rebar:ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb]' for RemoteRef but describes no update to that ADR or new ADR for the Protocol taxonomy.
The five role Protocols, three capability Protocols, and the contract-suite-as-backend-harness pattern are all net-new architectural decisions with no planned documentation artifact.
ABSENCE: no mention of 'ADR', 'AGENTS.md', or 'design doc' update in Scope or AC.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a net-new architectural pattern (five role Protocols + three capability Protocols + `RemoteRef` + contract-test harness as the canonical spec for future backends) but does not mention updating ADR 0035 or creating a new ADR/design-doc entry to capture these decisions. Future backend authors have no normative reference for which Protocols to implement, how capability opt-in works, or where the contract suite lives.
findings.location: Context / Problem (ADR 0035 reference), Scope section, Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A developer implementing a second backend (epic be74) has no normative spec — they must reverse-engineer Protocol semantics from the contract suite and source code.
If ADR 0035 is the canonical reference for RemoteRef but its §(d) is not updated to point to `_backend.py`, the ADR becomes stale relative to the implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'Update ADR 0035 §(d) to record that `RemoteRef` is now defined in `_backend.py` and to document the five role / three capability Protocol taxonomy and the contract-suite harness pattern as the normative spec for future backend implementations.' Add a corresponding AC: '`git diff` shows ADR 0035 (or a new child ADR) updated with the Protocol taxonomy and a pointer to the contract suite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'literal expected values captured from current behavior' with expected values derived from an independently-specified oracle (e.g., hand-authored from the Jira field-mapping spec, a known-good fixture reviewed by the domain author, or a property-based assertion about structure) so characterization tests assert correctness, not just current-output identity.
evidence: 'Characterization tests pin the exact current outputs of the pure Jira mappers … for representative tickets'
'verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty)'
Plan does not specify any independently-derived specification or business-rule oracle for the expected mapper outputs — only 'current behavior' is named as the source of truth
findings.finding: The characterization tests are explicitly described as capturing 'literal expected values … from current behavior' and verifying 'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty' — this is the self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern. Expected values derived by running the current implementation and pasting its output as the expected value lock in whatever the current mappers produce (including latent bugs), rather than asserting correctness against a specification. This is MAJOR per E5.
findings.location: Testing section (Characterization tests paragraph) and AC line 3: 'Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main for the representative fixtures — verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty).'
scenarios: A bug currently exists in _map_local_to_jira_fields (e.g. wrong priority key mapping). The characterization test captures the buggy output as the expected value. The test passes. JiraBackend ships, locking in the bug. The test provides a false safety signal.
A future refactor correctly fixes the mapper behavior; the characterization test goes RED for the wrong reason, misleading the engineer.
findings.suggested_fix: For each representative fixture, hand-author the expected output dict based on the documented field-mapping rules (e.g. the ADR or Jira field spec), not by running the current code and pasting its output. If the current output matches the spec, the test is redundant-but-safe; if it doesn't, the test now catches a real bug. Alternatively, frame these explicitly as regression-snapshot tests (accepted as a known limitation) and add at least one separately-specified correctness assertion per mapper (e.g., 'priority field maps to Jira priority name, not id') to give the suite real discriminating power.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence of positive rationale for the role-Protocol decomposition in the Approach section (e.g., why five roles instead of one flat Backend interface) so the implementer understands the boundary principle, not just the mapping.
evidence: Plan describes what each Protocol maps to (e.g., 'TicketTransport ← create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient') but not why these five roles are the correct decomposition
ADR ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb established the role-Protocol table, but the plan does not cite the rationale from the ADR (independent replaceability of roles, testability of individual concerns)
ABSENCE: no statement like 'role decomposition chosen because each role can be independently implemented/tested/replaced'
findings.finding: The plan states a positive rationale for the extract-don't-move approach but does not state a positive rationale for the role-Protocol decomposition (five role Protocols + three capability Protocols + one Backend facade) over the simpler alternative of a single flat Backend Protocol. A reader implementing this cannot confirm why the decomposition boundary was chosen here.
scenarios: An implementer uncertain about the role boundaries (e.g., whether FieldSanitizer belongs in TicketTransport or as its own role) has no in-plan guidance and may collapse or expand roles inconsistently

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
